does: This paper develops automated methods for red teaming language models by using other language models to generate adversarial test cases that elicit harmful outputs. The authors train a target LM to generate offensive or problematic responses to automatically-crafted test questions, then use an offensive content classifier to identify these failures at scale. They explore multiple generation strategies ranging from zero-shot prompting to reinforcement learning, systematically uncovering tens of thousands of offensive replies in a 280B parameter chatbot. Beyond offensive content, they demonstrate prompt engineering techniques that reveal additional harms including stereotypical group discussions, privacy leakage through generated phone numbers, and other safety failures across diverse harm categories.
connects: This work extends prior red teaming research that relied on expensive human annotation to write test cases, building on the recognized problem that LMs exhibit hard-to-predict harmful behaviors at deployment time. It connects to broader work on adversarial robustness and stress-testing of neural systems, adopting the strategy of using learned models to automatically generate challenging inputs. The paper relates to concurrent research on using LMs as tools for evaluating and improving themselves, and builds on classifier-based safety approaches by combining automated generation with automated detection. It engages with prompt engineering literature and reinforcement learning methods for controlled text generation, applying these techniques to the safety-critical domain of identifying model vulnerabilities.
enables: This work establishes scalable, automated red teaming as a practical alternative to human annotation, enabling systematic discovery of LM failure modes across diverse harm categories. It points toward iterative safety processes where LM-based generation and detection can continuously improve model safety before and potentially during deployment. The demonstrated ability to uncover specific harm types through prompt engineering suggests possibilities for systematically mapping out LM vulnerability landscapes. This automated approach opens directions for building safer LMs through adversarial training loops, developing better offensive content classifiers trained on LM-generated examples, and creating dynamic safety benchmarks that scale with model capabilities. The methodology could generalize to other evaluation tasks beyond safety and harms.
